title: The French Revolution: Liberty, Equality, Fraternity
objective: Students will be able to identify and explain the key causes, events, and ideals of the French Revolution by analyzing primary sources and constructing a thesis statement connecting Enlightenment ideas to revolutionary outcomes.
standards: NYS Global History and Geography Standards - Grade 10: Renaissance and Reformation connections to revolutionary thought
NYS SS RH.9-10.1: Cite specific textual evidence to support analysis of primary sources
NYS SS RH.9-10.2: Determine the central ideas or information of a primary source
NYS SS WHST.9-10.1: Write arguments focused on discipline-specific content
do_now:
DO NOW (5 minutes) - Visual Analysis with Real-World Connection

Part 1: Look at the political cartoon showing Parisian women marching to Versailles in October 1789 (the "Bread March"). Describe TWO specific details you observe in the image. (2 minutes)

Part 2: Based on what you see, what human actions might have caused these women to march ten miles to Versailles? Write your answer in complete sentences. (2 minutes)

Part 3: Imagine you are a Parisian baker in 1789. Bread prices have tripled, and your family cannot afford to eat. How would this crisis affect your daily life today, tomorrow, and over the next month? Provide at least two concrete examples for each timeframe. (1 minute)

Transition: "Excellent observations, historians! Today we're diving into one of history's most explosive transformations—the French Revolution. That bread crisis you just imagined? That was REAL for millions of French citizens. Let's find out why."
direct_instruction:
DIRECT INSTRUCTION (20 minutes) - I Do / We Do

[Setting the Stage - 3 minutes]
Alright historians, today we step into the shoes of French citizens in 1789. Your mission: figure out why ordinary people—men, women, and even children—risked their lives to tear down an entire system of government. Ready? Let's go.

[Context Slide - Show image of pre-revolutionary France inequality]
Before we analyze documents, let's establish the HISTORICAL CONTEXT. Historical context means the circumstances that surround an event—economic conditions, social structures, political situations, and cultural attitudes that help us understand WHY something happened.

[Direct Instruction Script:]
France in 1789 was a powder keg. Here's why:
- The King (Louis XVI) and nobility lived in extreme luxury while 90% of the population lived in poverty
- The feudal system kept peasants bound to land they didn't own
- The Catholic Church collected taxes AND held enormous political power
- France was BROKE from funding the American Revolution AND bad harvests
- The Enlightenment had spread ideas about natural rights, social contracts, and popular sovereignty

CHECK FOR UNDERSTANDING: Turn to your neighbor. In 30 seconds, explain why France was "a powder keg" in 1789. What had to happen for it to explode? [Circulate and listen for student responses]

[Document Analysis Introduction - 5 minutes]
Now, I'm going to distribute Document A—the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen, 1789. This is a PRIMARY SOURCE, meaning it was written DURING the time period we're studying. As we read, I want you to identify:
1. What RIGHTS does this document declare?
2. Where have we seen these ideas BEFORE? (Hint: Think Enlightenment philosophers)
3. Why would people RISK THEIR LIVES for these ideas?

[Read aloud with annotation]:

"Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen, 1789"

"The Representatives of the French People...have resolved to set forth in a solemn declaration the natural, unalienable, and sacred rights of man..."

[Pause] What does "unalienable" mean? It means rights that CANNOT be taken away. Think about that power!

"Article 1: Men are born and remain free and equal in rights..."

[Pause] BORN equal. Not made equal by government. This is a direct challenge to the King!

"Article 2: The purpose of government is to secure these natural rights...Liberty, Property, Security, and Resistance to Oppression."

[WE DO - Guided Analysis - 5 minutes]
Now, let's analyze this TOGETHER. On your INSPECT Chart (Interaction, Social, Political, Economic, Cultural, Technology), we're going to identify how this document represents CHANGE.

- POLITICAL: Before—kings claimed divine right to rule. After—this document says sovereignty comes from the PEOPLE.
- SOCIAL: Before—rigid class system. After—all men are equal in rights.
- ECONOMIC: Article 17 guarantees property rights—this protects the emerging middle class.

CHECK FOR UNDERSTANDING: Looking at your INSPECT Chart, which category shows the BIGGEST change? Be ready to defend your choice with TEXTUAL EVIDENCE from the document. [Cold call 2-3 students]

[Transition - 2 minutes]
"This document tells us WHAT the revolutionaries WANTED. But HOW did they get it? Let's examine the violent reality of revolution."
guided_practice:
GUIDED PRACTICE (15 minutes) - We Do / You Do

[Pair Work with Role Division - 10 minutes]
Alright, historians! Push your desks together to make an island with your partner. Yes, push them together NOW!

Person A: You will complete Document Analysis on the political cartoon showing the Storming of the Bastille (July 14, 1789).
Person B: You will complete Document Analysis on the excerpt from Edmund Burke's "Reflections on the Revolution in France" (1790), a contemporary critic.

Here are your GUIDING QUESTIONS for each document:

DOCUMENT A: Political Cartoon - Storming of the Bastille
1. What event does this image depict? When and where did it occur?
2. Identify TWO details showing crowd behavior. What does this reveal about popular revolution?
3. What does the Bastille represent symbolically? Why was its fall so significant?
4. CULMINATING: How does this image support or challenge the ideals from the Declaration of the Rights of Man?

DOCUMENT B: Edmund Burke's "Reflections on the Revolution in France" (1790)
"Society is indeed a contract...the state ought not to be regarded as nothing better than a partnership agreement in a trade of pepper and coffee...to be dissolved at the mere fancy of the parties."

1. What is Burke's main argument about society and government?
2. What does he mean by comparing society to a "partnership agreement in a trade of pepper and coffee"?
3. Identify ONE concern Burke has about revolutionary change.
4. CULMINATING: How does Burke's perspective challenge the Declaration of the Rights of Man we analyzed earlier?

[Share-Out Structure - 3 minutes]
When I say "SHARE," Person A has 60 seconds to summarize their document's key insight. Person B listens and asks ONE clarifying question. Then switch. I'll give you a timing bell.

[Circulate during pair work - look for:]
- Are students using TEXTUAL EVIDENCE from documents?
- Can they identify the connection between Enlightenment ideas and revolutionary actions?
- Are they engaging with the complexity of the documents?

[Whole-Class Debrief - 2 minutes]
So what did we learn? The French Revolution wasn't just about bread prices and angry mobs—it was about IDEAS. Enlightenment philosophers like Rousseau, Voltaire, and Montesquieu gave the French vocabulary to articulate their grievances AND a vision for a new society. The Declaration of Rights Man is essentially the Enlightenment made real.

But Burke reminds us: not everyone agreed. Revolution is messy. Change is dangerous. Sound familiar? [Pause for reflection]
independent_work:
INDEPENDENT WORK (10 minutes) - You Do

[Thesis Development with Writing Frame - 10 minutes]
Alright, historians! Time for you to demonstrate what you've learned. Clear your desks except for a pen or pencil.

Today you're going to practice the skill you'll need on the Regents—crafting a THESIS STATEMENT. A thesis is a clear, specific argument that answers the question AND previews your evidence.

Here's your TASK:
Based on our analysis of the Declaration of Rights of Man, the Storming of the Bastille, and Burke's critique, answer this question:

"To what extent did Enlightenment ideas cause the French Revolution?"

WRITING FRAME (sentence starters to help you):

The Enlightenment ideas caused the French Revolution primarily because ___________________________________
[Provide 2-3 specific examples from documents]
However, other factors such as ___________________________________ also contributed.
In conclusion, while Enlightenment ideas were [important/significant/crucial], the Revolution was ultimately caused by ___________________________________.

GRAPHIC ORGANIZER - Thesis Development T-Chart:

ENLIGHTENMENT IDEAS (Causes) OTHER FACTORS (Causes)
- Natural rights (Declaration) - Economic crisis (bread prices)
- Popular sovereignty (Article 3) - Feudal system exploitation
- Social contract theory - Bankruptcy of French government
- Resistance to oppression (Art. 2) - Glorious American Revolution example

Your job: Fill in the T-Chart with evidence, THEN construct your thesis using the writing frame above.

[Extension for Early Finishers]:
Congratulations! If you've completed your thesis, you've earned the chance to think like a Royal Advisor—but French style! If you were Marie Antoinette receiving this Declaration of Rights, what would be your GREATEST concern? Write a 30-second speech (4-5 sentences) as the Queen responding to the revolutionaries.

[Collection]: All students submit thesis statement and T-Chart at the end of class.

exit_ticket.question: According to the Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen, what THREE natural rights are guaranteed to all men? Quote from the document to support your answer.
exit_ticket.expected_response: Students should identify Article 2's "Liberty, Property, Security, and Resistance to Oppression" and cite specific articles (Article 1 - equality, Article 17 - property). Strong responses quote directly from the document.

exit_ticket.question: How did the Storming of the Bastille (Document A) demonstrate the connection between Enlightenment ideas and revolutionary action? Use specific evidence from both sources.
exit_ticket.expected_response: Students should explain that the Declaration's ideas about popular sovereignty and resistance to oppression motivated the crowd to storm the Bastille. The symbolic destruction of royal tyranny. Strong answers synthesize both documents.

exit_ticket.question: Edmund Burke criticized the French Revolution as dangerous and chaotic. Based on our analysis, construct a thesis statement explaining whether you agree or disagree that the Revolution was necessary given the conditions in France. Your thesis must be specific and include evidence from at least two sources we analyzed today.
exit_ticket.expected_response: Proficient thesis: "The French Revolution was necessary because [specific reason], as demonstrated by [evidence from Declaration/storming of Bastille], however Burke's critique of [specific concern] raises valid questions about [issue]." Responses should use the writing frame and show synthesis of multiple documents.

homework:
HOMEWORK ASSIGNMENT

Due Next Class:

"Letter from Versailles" - Primary Source Analysis

You are a servant working in the Palace of Versailles in October 1789. The Parisian women have just marched here demanding bread. Write a 1-page letter (minimum 250 words) to a family member in the countryside describing:

1. What you WITNESSED during the women's march to Versailles
2. How the King's response (agreeing to return to Paris) changes France's political power
3. Why bread prices and inequality led to revolution—use evidence from class
4. Your prediction: Will things improve or get worse? Why?

ACADEMIC VOCABULARY TO INCLUDE:
- Natural Rights
- Popular Sovereignty
- Feudal System
- Historical Context

CHALLENGE OPTION: Include a quote from the Declaration of Rights of Man and explain how it relates to what you witnessed.

This homework connects today's primary source analysis to historical empathy and document-based writing you'll need for the Regents.

struggling: Sentence starters provided for ALL written responses: 'The Enlightenment caused revolution because...' 'This document shows...' 'I believe the revolution was [necessary/unnecessary] because...'
Graphic organizer (T-Chart and INSPECT Chart) pre-filled with key vocabulary in bold, reducing cognitive load while maintaining rigor
Reduced exit ticket: Struggling learners complete Question 1 and 2 only, with additional sentence frames for Question 2 explaining cause/effect relationships between documents
advanced: Extension: Compare the French Declaration of Rights of Man to the American Declaration of Independence (1776). Write a thesis arguing which document had GREATER impact on establishing democratic principles globally, using specific evidence from both.

Challenge: Analyze Burke's full quote from today alongside Marx's later critique of the French Revolution. How do these two critics view revolution differently? Which critique do you find more persuasive, and why?

Enrichment: Research the role of Olympe de Gouges' "Declaration of the Rights of Woman and the Female Citizen" (1791). How does this document both affirm AND challenge the 1789 Declaration? Present your findings in a 2-minute Socratic-style discussion with classmates.
ell: Key vocabulary previewed with visual supports: Show images of Bastille, Versailles, guillotine, Declaration document BEFORE class begins; create visual word wall for terms: sovereignty, feudal, unalienable, resistance
Bilingual glossary available (French-English cognates): révolution = revolution, droits = rights, égalité = equality, propriété = property - these cognates support comprehension

Simplified sentence frames with academic vocabulary scaffolds: 'This document shows [concept] because [evidence].' 'The Enlightenment ideas of [philosopher] influenced [event] through [specific mechanism].'

materials_needed: Do Now handout with political cartoon of women's march to Versailles (1789)
Declaration of the Rights of Man and of the Citizen (1789) - full text excerpt with article numbers highlighted
Document A: Political cartoon of Storming of the Bastille (July 14, 1789)
Document B: Edmund Burke excerpt from "Reflections on the Revolution in France" (1790)
INSPECT Chart graphic organizer (pre-printed) - columns for Interactions, Social, Political, Economic, Cultural, Technology
T-Chart graphic organizer for thesis development (pre-printed)
Thesis Writing Frame handout (pre-printed with sentence starters)
Exit ticket half-sheets (3 questions per page)
Period coins for engagement (if using signature rewards system)
